title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a ranger called Alex talking about a residential conservation week in a mountain area. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a ranger called Alex talking about a residential conservation week in a mountain area. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A ranger called Alex introduces a residential conservation week.
extracts.audio_script: Hi everyone. I’m Alex, one of the rangers responsible for Highland Trail Conservation Week, a residential programme for students who want to work outdoors while protecting popular walking routes. The programme operates from a small centre beside the hills. The building has not always been a ranger base; it was originally a [9]sheep farm[/9], and one old stone building has been converted into sleeping accommodation. The project lasts five days and includes practical tasks, training and time to explore short routes safely with staff.||People often think our main job is removing litter. That matters, but walkers also need clearly maintained paths which prevent erosion across wider areas of hillside. This year our priority is repairing a route leading to a [10]waterfall[/10], because heavy winter rain has washed away parts of the surface and visitors have begun stepping around muddy sections. Students first attend a briefing about [11]mountain weather[/11], learning why plans may change quickly and why conditions lower down do not always show what the hills will be like.||Participants live and eat at the centre. Bedrooms are shared and bedding is provided. Students should bring a towel, walking clothes and a [12]head torch[/12], because the path between the sleeping building and dining room is dark in the evening. The first practical job is suitable for beginners: under supervision, students help [13]move stones[/13] to areas where the damaged walking route will be rebuilt. More technical work, such as placing stones correctly or constructing drainage channels, is completed by experienced rangers. Gloves and all heavy equipment are supplied.||The programme involves learning about local nature as well as repairing paths. During one afternoon, students use maps to record areas where [14]wild flowers[/14] are growing beside routes, so future repair work avoids unnecessary damage. We also offer one optional evening activity. Last year it was a film about climbing. This year, students voted most strongly for a [15]navigation workshop[/15], where they will practise reading contours and planning simple safe walks indoors before trying a short exercise outside. This is useful even for people who do not plan to walk alone.||At the end of the week, each student creates a short [16]project diary[/16], including photographs, observations and a description of work completed. They also receive a [17]conservation certificate[/17] from the park authority. Our programme currently welcomes small groups only because we do not have enough space for wet clothing and equipment. Money from this autumn's sponsored hill walk will be used to build a new [18]drying room[/18] beside the accommodation block, making future residential visits more comfortable. Places for this summer are limited, and applications should be submitted by the end of next month.

dialogue.speaker: alex
dialogue.text: Hi everyone. I’m Alex, one of the rangers responsible for Highland Trail Conservation Week, a residential programme for students who want to work outdoors while protecting popular walking routes. The programme operates from a small centre beside the hills. The building has not always been a ranger base; it was originally a sheep farm, and one old stone building has been converted into sleeping accommodation. The project lasts five days and includes practical tasks, training and time to explore short routes safely with staff.||People often think our main job is removing litter. That matters, but walkers also need clearly maintained paths which prevent erosion across wider areas of hillside. This year our priority is repairing a route leading to a waterfall, because heavy winter rain has washed away parts of the surface and visitors have begun stepping around muddy sections. Students first attend a briefing about mountain weather, learning why plans may change quickly and why conditions lower down do not always show what the hills will be like.||Participants live and eat at the centre. Bedrooms are shared and bedding is provided. Students should bring a towel, walking clothes and a head torch, because the path between the sleeping building and dining room is dark in the evening. The first practical job is suitable for beginners: under supervision, students help move stones to areas where the damaged walking route will be rebuilt. More technical work, such as placing stones correctly or constructing drainage channels, is completed by experienced rangers. Gloves and all heavy equipment are supplied.||The programme involves learning about local nature as well as repairing paths. During one afternoon, students use maps to record areas where wild flowers are growing beside routes, so future repair work avoids unnecessary damage. We also offer one optional evening activity. Last year it was a film about climbing. This year, students voted most strongly for a navigation workshop, where they will practise reading contours and planning simple safe walks indoors before trying a short exercise outside. This is useful even for people who do not plan to walk alone.||At the end of the week, each student creates a short project diary, including photographs, observations and a description of work completed. They also receive a conservation certificate from the park authority. Our programme currently welcomes small groups only because we do not have enough space for wet clothing and equipment. Money from this autumn's sponsored hill walk will be used to build a new drying room beside the accommodation block, making future residential visits more comfortable. Places for this summer are limited, and applications should be submitted by the end of next month.
